labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

labels.name: wontfix
labels.description: This will not be worked on

issues.title: Redirect to app should use deep link
issues.author: Mawdie
issues.body:
Hi,

I think it should say `deep_link` here.

https://github.com/yaron-zehavi/oauth-native-clients-federation/blob/04f2a01dad8e0fc309b9cc7b6b04a33d731c5453/draft-zehavi-oauth-native-clients-federation.md?plain=1#L214

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
Right you are!
Thanks for noticing and letting me know.
Out of curiosity, may I ask what's your interest in this draft? Do you have a use case for it?
Do you have additional feedback?

issues.title: insufficient_information needs a warning/prohibition about use with passwords/otps with third party apps
issues.author: jogu
issues.body:
The `insufficient_information` response is really quite generic and open to potential abuse:

https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zehavi-oauth-native-clients-federation-01.html#name-additional-information-requ

e.g. if a password was requested it's kind of reinvented the resource open password credentials, and there'd be similar concerns with a third party app asking the user for an otp.

I think it should likely prohibit use of it for anything other than information necessary to identity the user and their applicable authorization server, with actual authentication not being allowed. Or something like that - not sure exactly what is the correct nuance.

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
Thanks Joseph!
I appreciate your feedback.

I think the issue you mention arises mostly from **free text inputs**, when (rogue) AS controls **title, hint and description**.
A possible solution would be to normatively specify an allow-list of free text input **types**, with clients rejecting anything not on the list. For these types fixed **title, hint and description** shall be defined, which clients may override for localization purposes.

At first propose to include **email & phone** as only free text input types.
Other use-cases may extend in future using profiles.

comments.author: jogu
comments.body:
I think just email & phone to start with makes sense and avoids the issue. I think I'd still add a statement along the lines 'For third party apps, this mechanism MUST NOT be extended to request user credentials like passwords, OTPs, etc'.

The problem isn't just rogue ASes - it's generally a bad pattern for the user to enter their password into a third party app (because it means the third party app has access to the user's password, which is/can be a far more powerful credential than the access token we're trying to give them), and an even worse pattern for the AS to actually be encouraging it. i.e. same reason we use in-app browser tabs instead of web view, same reason resource owner password credentials grant was dropped.

comments.author: yaron-zehavi
comments.body:
I don't mind adding statements, was rather looking for actionable steps preventing misuse altogether.

The security considerations of this draft deals with AS considering and perhaps avoiding asking users for sensitive data through a native client who's being federated (I think that's what you mean by third party app?)
https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zehavi-oauth-native-clients-federation-01.html#name-non-first-party-application

Will consider adding language to insufficient_information response to clarify the matter.
Please note draft already defines this response: "the Authorization Server requires additional user input, _other than an authentication challenge_".
If expanding it, perhaps use the latter part of your sentence: "...this mechanism MUST NOT be extended to request user credentials like passwords, OTPs, etc", as your comment is relevant not only for third party apps.

Keep in mind though that not all authentication challenges are sensitive, a fido webauthn credential request, DC presentation request etc are in my view non-sensitive challenges that also a third party app can serve. Do you agree?

issues.title: audience of authorization_code from redirect_to_app response
issues.author: yaron-zehavi
issues.body:
Draft is currently confusing about the audience of authorization_code from redirect_to_app response.
Originally it said code should be returned directly to federating AS.
Then if there's no federation added a comment authorization_code goes to authorization server which instructed redirect_to_app.

I think this should be simplified so that audience of authorization_code is always the last (current) authorization server.
This makes the redirect_to_app response equivalent to authorization challenges.
